title: Mixed conditionals, wish and if only
lesson: Mixed conditionals combine two time frames. Past condition to present result: 'If + past perfect, ... would + base verb' (If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now). Present condition to past result: 'If + past simple, ... would have + past participle' (If I weren't so shy, I would have spoken to her). For regrets, use 'wish/if only + past perfect' about the past (I wish I had known) and 'wish/if only + past simple' about the present (I wish I were taller); 'wish + would' is used for annoying habits or wanted change (I wish you would stop).

items.text: If I had taken that job in Paris, I {1} (live) there now.

blanks.hint: past condition, present result: would + base / would be -ing

items.explanation: Past condition ('had taken') with a present result uses 'would + base verb' (or 'would be living') because the result is happening now.

items.text: If she weren't so afraid of flying, she {1} (come) with us to Japan last year.

blanks.hint: present condition, past result: would have + past participle

items.explanation: A present, ongoing condition ('weren't so afraid') with a past result uses 'would have + past participle'.

items.text: If he hadn't missed the train, he {1} (be) at the meeting right now.

blanks.hint: past condition, present result: would + base

items.explanation: The past condition ('hadn't missed') produces a present result ('right now'), so we use 'would + base verb'.

items.text: If I spoke fluent German, I {1} (get) that contract in Berlin two months ago.

blanks.hint: present condition, past result: would have + past participle

items.explanation: A general present condition ('spoke fluent German') with a past result ('two months ago') uses 'would have + past participle'.

items.text: If they had saved more money when they were young, they {1} (not worry) about bills today.
items.explanation: The past condition ('had saved') leads to a present result ('today'), so we use 'wouldn't worry' / 'wouldn't be worrying'.

items.text: If I weren't working tomorrow, I {1} (join) you on the trip.

blanks.hint: present/future condition, present result: would + base

items.explanation: A present arrangement ('weren't working') with a present/future result uses 'would + base verb'.

items.text: I wish I {1} (study) harder when I was at school.

blanks.hint: wish + past perfect for a past regret

items.explanation: To express a regret about the past, we use 'wish + past perfect'.

items.text: I wish I {1} (be) taller; I can never reach the top shelf.

blanks.hint: wish + past simple for the present; 'were' is preferred

items.explanation: To express a wish about a present situation, we use 'wish + past simple'; 'were' is preferred for all persons, though 'was' is common in informal speech.

items.text: If only I {1} (not say) those hurtful words at the party last night.

blanks.hint: if only + past perfect for a past regret

items.explanation: 'If only', like 'wish', takes the past perfect when we regret a past action.

items.text: I wish you {1} (stop) interrupting me every time I try to speak.

blanks.hint: wish + would for an annoying habit

items.explanation: 'Wish + would' is used to complain about an annoying habit or to express a desire for someone's behaviour to change.

items.text: If only it {1} (not rain) so much here; the garden is always flooded.

blanks.hint: if only + past simple about the present

items.explanation: 'If only + past simple' expresses a wish about a present, ongoing situation; 'wouldn't rain' is also acceptable for a wanted change.

items.text: If we had bought that house ten years ago, we {1} (not pay) so much rent now.
items.explanation: The past condition ('had bought') leads to a present result ('now'), so the main clause uses 'wouldn't pay' / 'wouldn't be paying'.
